stem: You are the paediatric resident reviewing an 11-year-old girl with four weeks of polyuria, polydipsia and 4 kg of unintentional weight loss. She has had no vomiting and is alert and well perfused. Her family has no history of diabetes. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. Her mother is keen to take her home if it is safe.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 92, BP 110/68, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Well perfused, no acetone breath, no Kussmaul breathing, soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: BSL 22 mmol/L, capillary ketones 1.4 mmol/L, venous pH 7.36, HCO3 22 mmol/L, Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 58 micromol/L, HbA1c 11.6%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Give a 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline and commence an intravenous insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/hour with hourly capillary glucose monitoring
options.rationale: An insulin infusion is the diabetic ketoacidosis pathway and is not indicated when venous pH and bicarbonate are normal and ketones are only mildly elevated; it carries unnecessary risk of hypoglycaemia and hypokalaemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes Mellitus)

options.text: Admit for intravenous maintenance fluids and oral fluids only with insulin deferred until capillary ketones have cleared below 0.6 mmol/L
options.rationale: Withholding insulin in new-onset hyperglycaemia perpetuates ketogenesis and lipolysis and will worsen the metabolic picture; insulin is the definitive treatment for hyperglycaemia with ketosis. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Guidelines)

options.text: Admit under the paediatric diabetes team for subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin initiation, structured education and a written sick-day plan before discharge
options.rationale: New-onset hyperglycaemia without acidosis is managed with subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin and inpatient education by a paediatric diabetes service; this is the Australian standard and prevents readmission with diabetic ketoacidosis. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Guidelines)

options.text: Discharge on metformin 500 mg twice daily with outpatient paediatric endocrinology review and home glucose monitoring within one week
options.rationale: Metformin is not appropriate for new-onset type 1 diabetes; the clinical picture of rapid weight loss and ketones points to insulin deficiency, and oral hypoglycaemics will not arrest progression to ketoacidosis. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Guidelines)

options.text: Start subcutaneous long-acting insulin glargine alone with discharge home and outpatient diabetes review arranged for 48 hours later
options.rationale: Basal insulin alone will not cover prandial glycaemic excursions and ketosis, and discharging without structured education and a sick-day plan risks early readmission with diabetic ketoacidosis. (Source: APEG Type 1 Diabetes Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Around a third of paediatric type 1 diabetes presentations occur without ketoacidosis. These children still require hospital admission for insulin initiation because the underlying insulin deficiency will progress without treatment. Inpatient education by the paediatric diabetes team covers dose adjustment, hypoglycaemia recognition, ketone monitoring and sick-day rules. Discharge without these supports is a leading cause of early readmission with diabetic ketoacidosis.
key_points: Diabetic ketoacidosis is defined by venous pH below 7.3 or bicarbonate below 15 with ketones above 3.0 mmol/L
Subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin is the initial regimen for non-ketoacidotic presentations in Australia
Inpatient diabetes education is part of initial management, not an optional add-on
Metformin and oral hypoglycaemics have no role in type 1 diabetes initiation
A documented sick-day plan with ketone testing thresholds is required before discharge
explanation.pearls: Capillary ketones above 0.6 mmol/L with a glucose above 14 mmol/L on a sick day triggers extra rapid-acting insulin every 2 hours under the APEG sick-day rule.

subtopic_detail: Iron deficiency anaemia in a toddler
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 22-month-old boy whose parents are concerned that he is pale and tires easily. He drinks around 900 mL of cow's milk daily, refuses meat and prefers crackers. He was born at term and is otherwise well. There is no history of jaundice, dark urine or recent infection. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 116, RR 28, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no scleral icterus, no hepatosplenomegaly, soft systolic flow murmur
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L, MCV 64 fL, MCH 19 pg, RDW 17.5%, platelets 480 x10^9/L, ferritin 6 micrograms/L, CRP 3 mg/L, reticulocyte count 0.7%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange transfusion of packed red cells in the paediatric day unit and refer to paediatric haematology for further evaluation
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia or haemoglobin below 70 g/L with cardiovascular compromise; this child is haemodynamically stable and responds to oral iron. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Start oral ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg of elemental iron once daily, reduce cow's milk to under 500 mL/day and review with full blood count in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Excess cow's milk intake displaces iron-rich foods and causes occult gut blood loss; oral iron with dietary modification corrects iron deficiency anaemia and the haemoglobin should rise by at least 10 g/L within a month. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Order haemoglobin electrophoresis, direct antiglobulin test and serum lactate dehydrogenase before starting any treatment to exclude haemoglobinopathy
options.rationale: The ferritin, microcytosis and dietary history are diagnostic of iron deficiency in this context; further haematology workup is reserved for failure to respond to a trial of oral iron. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Refer for an intravenous iron infusion in the outpatient setting now and continue current dietary intake while awaiting the appointment
options.rationale: Intravenous iron is reserved for malabsorption, oral iron intolerance or treatment failure; first-line therapy in a well toddler is oral iron with dietary adjustment. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Reassure the family that the findings reflect a physiological dietary phase and review in three months without starting any pharmacological treatment
options.rationale: A ferritin under 12 micrograms/L with microcytic anaemia is not physiological and untreated iron deficiency in toddlers causes long-term neurodevelopmental harm. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency anaemia is the most common nutritional deficiency in Australian toddlers, driven by excessive cow's milk intake and selective eating. Oral elemental iron at 3 mg/kg once daily with dietary modification corrects the deficit in the majority of well children. A rise in haemoglobin of at least 10 g/L by four weeks confirms response; failure to respond prompts review for malabsorption, ongoing blood loss or alternative diagnoses. Persistent iron deficiency in this age group is associated with cognitive and motor delay.
key_points: Cow's milk above 500 mL daily in toddlers displaces dietary iron and causes occult enteric blood loss
Ferritin under 12 micrograms/L is diagnostic of iron deficiency in children when the C-reactive protein is normal
Oral iron once daily improves absorption versus divided dosing because of hepcidin kinetics
Reticulocytosis at 7 to 10 days confirms early response and haemoglobin rise at 4 weeks confirms cure
Intravenous iron is second line and reserved for treatment failure or intolerance
explanation.pearls: Once-daily oral iron given on alternate days may improve fractional absorption versus thrice-daily dosing because hepcidin remains elevated for 24 hours after each dose.

subtopic_detail: Moderate atopic eczema flare topical step-up
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 3-year-old girl with a one-week flare of itchy, weeping eczema on her cheeks and antecubital fossae. She has been using a daily soap substitute and 1% hydrocortisone for two weeks without improvement. There is no honey-coloured crust, no fever and no spreading erythema. She is otherwise well and has no allergies. The parents are reluctant to use stronger topical steroids.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 104, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Erythematous, weeping eczema on face and flexures, lichenification at popliteal fossae, no pustules or crusting, no lymphadenopathy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Continue 1% hydrocortisone twice daily indefinitely with daily bleach baths and refer to a paediatric dermatologist for biologic therapy assessment
options.rationale: Failure of 1% hydrocortisone in a moderate flare warrants topical step-up rather than indefinite continuation; biologics are reserved for severe disease refractory to standard topical care. (Source: ACD Atopic Dermatitis Position Statement)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for five days and review in clinic at the end of the course before tapering the dose
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids cause rebound flares on cessation and are not used for atopic eczema in children outside specialist supervision. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

options.text: Commence oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg three times daily empirically for ten days and continue current topical regimen unchanged
options.rationale: There is no honey-coloured crust, pustule or systemic feature to suggest staphylococcal superinfection; antibiotics will not address the underlying inflammatory flare. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

options.text: Step up to methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment once daily for two weeks, continue emollients and soap substitutes and review at the end of the course
options.rationale: A moderate-potency topical corticosteroid is the next step when low-potency steroid fails; a defined two-week course with emollients restores skin barrier function and matches Australian dermatology advice. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

options.text: Trial topical pimecrolimus 1% cream twice daily without any topical corticosteroid for a fortnight and review the response in clinic at that point
options.rationale: Topical calcineurin inhibitors are second line for steroid-sparing maintenance or for sensitive sites such as eyelids, not for inducing remission in an actively weeping flare. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

explanation.summary: Atopic eczema management is a stepped ladder anchored on emollients and soap substitutes, with topical corticosteroid potency matched to flare severity. Hydrocortisone 1% is sufficient for mild flares on the face; moderate body or flexural flares need a moderate-potency steroid such as methylprednisolone aceponate. A short defined course of stronger steroid is safer and more effective than prolonged low-potency steroid. Parental steroid phobia is a major driver of undertreatment and persistent flare.
key_points: Topical corticosteroid potency should match flare severity, not patient age in isolation
Methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% is a moderate-potency steroid safe for short courses in children
Antibiotics are added only when bacterial superinfection is clinically suspected
Topical calcineurin inhibitors are second line, particularly for sensitive sites or maintenance
Systemic corticosteroids are avoided because of rebound flares on withdrawal
explanation.pearls: The fingertip unit covers one adult fingertip from the distal interphalangeal joint to the tip and treats an area equal to two adult palms; this gives parents a reproducible dosing measure.

subtopic_detail: Urinary tract infection empirical antibiotic in a young child
stem: You are the emergency department registrar assessing a 14-month-old girl with 2 days of fever and irritability. She is feeding poorly but is alert and tracking carers. There is no cough, coryza or rash. She has no allergies and has had no recent antibiotics. A clean-catch urine sample has been collected. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2 °C, HR 158, RR 32, SpO2 99% room air, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, clear chest, soft abdomen, no rash, ears and throat normal
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: leucocytes 3+, nitrites positive, blood trace, protein 1+. Urine microscopy and culture pending.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical management while awaiting urine culture?

options.text: Start oral trimethoprim plus sulfamethoxazole at standard paediatric dosing for 7 days and arrange follow-up with the GP at the end of the course
options.rationale: Trimethoprim with sulfamethoxazole is first-line empirical oral therapy for uncomplicated paediatric urinary tract infection in Australia, with a 7-day course in children under 2 years; she is well enough for oral therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit for intravenous gentamicin and benzylpenicillin pending the urine culture result and reassess for oral switch at 48 hours of treatment
options.rationale: Intravenous therapy is reserved for infants under 3 months, suspected pyelonephritis with sepsis, vomiting or inability to tolerate oral antibiotics; this child is well perfused and tolerating oral intake. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until urine culture confirms a uropathogen growth above 10^8 colony-forming units per litre to avoid driving resistance
options.rationale: Delaying treatment while waiting for a culture result risks progression to pyelonephritis or sepsis and is not consistent with Australian paediatric urinary tract infection management. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

options.text: Start oral amoxicillin alone at standard paediatric dosing for 5 days with planned follow-up at the GP at the end of the antibiotic course
options.rationale: Amoxicillin alone has unacceptably high resistance among community Escherichia coli isolates in Australia and is not first-line empirical therapy for paediatric urinary tract infection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Reassure the family and arrange a repeat urine specimen at the GP in 48 hours with antibiotics deferred until microscopy returns positive findings
options.rationale: A positive nitrite and leucocyte dipstick in a febrile child under 2 years is sufficient to start empirical antibiotics; deferring treatment risks progression. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary Tract Infection)

explanation.summary: Febrile urinary tract infection in young children is treated empirically once a clean-catch urine sample has been collected, because untreated infection risks renal scarring. Oral trimethoprim with sulfamethoxazole or cefalexin are the Australian first-line agents for clinically uncomplicated cases; intravenous therapy is reserved for infants under 3 months, sepsis or vomiting. A urine culture is sent to refine therapy at 48 hours and to identify resistant organisms. Imaging is indicated after a first febrile urinary tract infection in this age group.
key_points: Trimethoprim with sulfamethoxazole is first-line oral empirical therapy in Australian children over 1 month
Amoxicillin alone is not recommended because of high Escherichia coli resistance rates
Intravenous antibiotics are reserved for infants under 3 months, sepsis or oral intolerance
Renal tract ultrasound is recommended after a first febrile urinary tract infection in children under 2 years
Treatment duration is 7 days in children under 2 and may be 3 days in older children with cystitis
explanation.pearls: Recurrent febrile urinary tract infections or abnormal initial ultrasound trigger a micturating cystourethrogram or dimercaptosuccinic acid scan to look for vesicoureteric reflux and renal scarring.

subtopic_detail: Henoch-Schoenlein purpura with abdominal pain and normal renal function
stem: You are the paediatric resident reviewing a 6-year-old boy who has had a non-blanching palpable purpuric rash over his buttocks and lower legs for five days. He now has colicky periumbilical pain and one episode of bile-stained vomiting. He has had a cold two weeks before this presentation. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. He has no oedema and is passing normal urine volumes.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 °C, HR 110, BP 102/64, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura over buttocks and lower limbs, swollen tender ankles, soft abdomen with periumbilical tenderness, no peritonism, no scrotal swelling
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: blood 2+, protein 1+, no nitrites or leucocytes. Urine protein:creatinine 32 mg/mmol. Creatinine 32 micromol/L, albumin 38 g/L. FBC and CRP normal. Abdominal ultrasound shows thickened proximal small bowel without intussusception.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to paediatric surgery for emergent laparotomy because of bile-stained vomiting and abdominal pain in a child with palpable purpura
options.rationale: Bowel wall oedema from the vasculitis can mimic obstruction, but the ultrasound excludes intussusception and there is no peritonism; laparotomy is not indicated without surgical findings. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schoenlein Purpura)

options.text: Start intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg daily for three days and admit to the paediatric intensive care unit for monitoring
options.rationale: Pulse intravenous methylprednisolone is reserved for severe nephritis with crescents or rapidly progressive renal failure; this child has only mild proteinuria and no acute kidney injury. (Source: KHA-CARI HSP Nephritis Guideline)

options.text: Reassure the family that this is a self-limiting illness and discharge home with simple analgesia and outpatient follow-up arranged in six weeks
options.rationale: Significant abdominal pain warrants admission for analgesia, hydration and observation for intussusception; ongoing renal surveillance is also required to detect late nephritis. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schoenlein Purpura)

options.text: Initiate oral cyclophosphamide and azathioprine in conjunction with prednisolone and refer to paediatric nephrology for outpatient review within one week
options.rationale: Cytotoxic therapy is reserved for biopsy-proven severe nephritis; it is not indicated for abdominal pain with mild proteinuria. (Source: KHA-CARI HSP Nephritis Guideline)

options.text: Admit for analgesia, intravenous fluids and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for two weeks with weekly urinalysis and blood pressure monitoring for six months
options.rationale: Significant abdominal pain in vasculitis is shortened by oral corticosteroids without changing renal outcomes; weekly surveillance for nephritis is required because renal involvement may emerge over the following 6 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schoenlein Purpura)

explanation.summary: Henoch-Schoenlein purpura is an immunoglobulin A small-vessel vasculitis that follows an upper respiratory infection in school-aged children. The classical tetrad is palpable purpura, arthralgia, abdominal pain and renal involvement. Significant abdominal pain warrants admission for analgesia and observation for intussusception, which is typically ileoileal in this disease. Corticosteroids shorten the abdominal pain but do not alter the renal trajectory; long-term surveillance is mandated because nephritis can emerge over months.
key_points: Palpable purpura over buttocks and lower limbs with arthralgia is highly suggestive of HSP
Intussusception in HSP is typically ileoileal rather than ileocolic and may be missed by enema
Significant abdominal pain is shortened by oral prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg daily
Steroids do not prevent or alter the course of HSP nephritis
Weekly urinalysis and blood pressure for 6 months is required to detect late nephritis
explanation.pearls: Renal involvement may appear up to 6 months after the initial rash, so surveillance with urinalysis and blood pressure is continued even after symptoms resolve.
